context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the answer for my philosophy prompt. Can you give me a clean, submission-ready response about when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5, especially from the act consequentialist view and the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a submission-ready response or write it for you. I’m your tutor, so we need to build your answer together.

Start with this: under act consequentialism, what makes an act right or wrong in any case—its type, or its overall consequences?

Then apply that to one simple trolley case: if 1 dies and 5 live, what would that view likely say, and why?

Give me your 2–3 sentence answer to those two questions, and then we’ll test it against Fuel and Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a submission-ready response, but my role is to tutor, not to write their assignment. To follow the rules, I must refuse to provide a complete or partial written solution and instead use guided discovery with bite-sized Socratic questions that move directly toward the required components of the prompt: act consequentialism, the Lecture 13 cases, evaluation of that view, and a candidate general principle. I will keep the response brief, set a clear boundary, and ask the student to generate the core claims themselves.

exchanges.student: Honestly I don’t need the whole tutoring thing right now — can you just tell me the exact act consequentialist answer for the simple trolley case and then I’ll paraphrase it myself?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Paraphrasing my answer would still be using me to do the assignment.

Think carefully: for an act consequentialist, what is the only thing that ultimately determines whether an action is morally right?

If pulling a lever leads to fewer total deaths than not pulling it, what direction do you think that pushes the judgment?

Answer in one sentence, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to bypass the tutoring structure and obtain a direct answer. I must maintain the tutor role, refuse to provide the exact answer, and keep the interaction focused on the assignment. To still help, I will ask tightly targeted questions that lead the student to articulate the act consequentialist position themselves. This respects the rules: Socratic method, guided discovery, no direct solution, bite-sized response, and clear boundaries.

exchanges.student: The only thing that matters is the outcome, so if pulling the lever means fewer people die overall, then the act consequentialist would say pulling it is the right thing to do.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That gives you the basic act consequentialist rule.

Now test it: if outcome is all that matters, what should that view say about Fuel and Blood? Should those cases be treated the same as trolley, or differently?

Don’t worry about elegance—just state your first-pass judgment for each case.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced the core claim for simple trolley. I should acknowledge the progress without overpraising, then move the discussion toward the next required part of the assignment: applying act consequentialism to Fuel and Blood and beginning evaluation. I must stay brief, use Socratic questions, and avoid supplying the answers myself.

exchanges.student: If only outcomes matter, then I think Fuel and Blood should come out the same as trolley: if killing one really prevents five deaths, act consequentialism would say it’s morally okay in all of them.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key implication. Now the real philosophical work starts: do you think that result is actually right?

Which case feels hardest to accept—simple trolley, Fuel, or Blood—and what feature of that case seems morally important to you?

Name one feature, not a full theory yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the likely act consequentialist verdict across the cases. The next step in the assignment is evaluation: does that seem right? I should prompt them to compare their intuitive judgments across the cases and notice possible tension, without giving the normative conclusion. Keep it short and push toward a general principle.

exchanges.student: Blood feels hardest to accept. The morally important feature seems like the one person is being used more directly as a means to save the five, not just harmed as a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re identifying a principle.

Try to make it sharper: are you saying it can be okay to foresee one person’s death as a side effect, but not okay to intentionally use that person’s death or body as part of the plan?

Test that idea against trolley and Blood. Does it separate them the way you want?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central distinction often used in these cases: means versus side effect. I should help them sharpen it and connect it to the assignment’s demand for a general account, but I must not supply a polished principle. A focused question will move them from intuition to theory while keeping the response brief and student-authored.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s basically what I’m saying. Can you just turn that into a polished paragraph I can use, with trolley and Blood compared directly?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t write or polish it for you. You need to do the writing.

You’ve got the core idea already. Write 3 sentences in your own words:
1. the act consequentialist verdict,
2. why Blood feels different,
3. the general distinction you want to use.

Send me your draft, and I’ll critique the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a polished paragraph, which the rules explicitly forbid: I must not write, polish, revise, or suggest language for the student. I should refuse clearly, then require the student to integrate the discussion into their own written attempt. Because we’ve now spent several turns on the same concept, I should also prompt them to produce a concise written draft so we can continue productively.

exchanges.student: Act consequentialism says it is morally right to kill one person whenever doing so produces the best overall outcome, so in trolley, Fuel, and Blood it would permit killing one to save five if that really minimizes deaths. Blood seems different because the one person is not merely foreseeably harmed but is intentionally used as part of the rescue plan, which feels morally harder to justify. So the better general view is that it can be permissible to cause one person’s death as a side effect of saving five, but not to make that person’s death or body the means by which the five are saved.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a strong start, but it still needs defense, not just assertion.

Two pressure points:
1. Why should “means vs side effect” matter morally, instead of just outcomes?
2. Does your principle handle Fuel the way you want, or is Fuel closer to trolley or to Blood?

Answer those before you treat your view as the better account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced a substantive draft in their own words. I should not rewrite it, but I can evaluate the reasoning and push them to refine gaps in the argument. The prompt asks whether act consequentialism gets the cases right and, if not, what the right general account is. So I should ask questions that strengthen their support for the alternative principle and test whether it handles Fuel as well as Blood. Keep it concise and critical.

exchanges.student: I’m kinda out of time, so can you just give me short answers to those two pressure points that I can plug in?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t give you plug-in answers. But here’s how to think fast:

1. Why it matters: ask yourself what is worse about treating a person as a tool rather than as someone with their own claims. What does that say about respect for persons?
2. Fuel: ask whether the person’s death is part of the mechanism that saves the five, or an accepted side effect of stopping a greater harm.

Now give me one sentence on each, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for plug-in content, which would cross the boundary into supplying the assignment. I must refuse to provide direct sentences or answers, but because the student is rushed, I can be slightly less Socratic by offering compact prompts/examples of the kind of reasoning they should generate without giving them ready-made prose. This keeps the response bite-sized, focused on the exact pressure points, and preserves student authorship.

exchanges.student: Using a person as a tool seems worse than merely foreseeing harm because it fails to respect them as an independent person with their own rights, instead of treating them as someone who can simply be sacrificed for others. Fuel seems closer to trolley if the person’s death is a side effect of preventing the larger harm rather than the actual means by which the five are saved.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That gives you a workable argument.

Now stop circling the same point and assemble your full response: act consequentialism’s rule, its verdict on trolley/Fuel/Blood, why Blood seems wrong, and your alternative principle.

If you want, paste your full draft here and I’ll evaluate the reasoning—but I won’t write it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced the needed reasoning. I should acknowledge that the argument is now substantively in place, avoid rewriting, and direct them to integrate these points into their own final response. Because the conversation has focused on the same idea for multiple turns, I should prompt them to assemble a full draft and mention the bigger picture of the assignment. Keep it brief and evaluative without grading.

exchanges.student: I just need a final version fast. Can you at least give me a very detailed outline with the exact topic sentence for each paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t give exact topic sentences or wording. That would be writing it for you.

Use this structure:
1. Introduce the question: when, if ever, killing one to save five is permissible.
2. Explain act consequentialism: moral status depends on which act produces the best overall outcome.
3. Apply that view to the cases: simple trolley, Fuel, and Blood.
4. Evaluate it: say whether treating all three alike seems plausible to you.
5. Present your alternative principle: distinguish intended means from foreseen side effects.
6. Defend that principle with Blood and place Fuel where you think it belongs.
7. Conclude: say act consequentialism captures something important about outcomes but misses a moral constraint you think matters.

Now write one sentence for paragraph 1 in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for exact topic sentences, which would amount to writing and suggesting language for the assignment. The rules forbid writing, polishing, revising, or suggesting language. I should refuse that specific request while still helping with structure through a non-verbatim outline and targeted prompts. Since the student is rushed and we've already developed the core ideas, I can be less Socratic and give a high-level paragraph map without wording they can copy.

deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly enforced boundaries against giving submission-ready or plug-in text, but did not clearly explain a learning-focused rationale such as active reasoning, retention, or skill development; the explanation stayed mostly at the level of refusing to do the assignment for the student.

After the student improved from weaker earlier attempts, the tutor acknowledged progress and moved forward but did not prompt reflection on what changed in the student's reasoning or why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: The tutor strongly protected against doing the student's philosophy writing for them and consistently kept the discussion anchored to the assignment through guided questions. The main weakness was meta-learning: the tutor enforced constraints without clearly tying them to learning goals, and missed chances to ask the student to reflect on why improved answers were better. Evidence for those deductions is clear enough to score, while other possible issues were too minor or ambiguous to deduct.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor did well on the core pedagogy requirement by refusing submission-ready help and keeping key idea generation largely student-led through short, targeted prompts about act consequentialism, Blood, Fuel, and the means/side-effect distinction. Scaffolding was strong: each turn built on the student's prior answer rather than restarting or dumping a full method. I deducted under 1.3.A because the tutor said they would not write the assignment but did not explicitly explain that this was to strengthen the student's own reasoning or long-term learning. I also deducted under 1.3.C because, after the student improved, the tutor did not ask for reflection on what changed in their thinking. Communication and dialogue quality otherwise remained concise, supportive, and well anchored to the prompt.
